title: Cat Sneezing Blood Droplets: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat is sneezing blood droplets, the urgency level of this symptom, and how to prepare for your veterinary visit.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat is sneezing blood, it indicates a compromised nasal lining and requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if the bleeding is continuous or if your cat has trouble breathing.

What does it mean if my cat is sneezing blood?

When a cat sneezes and releases blood droplets, it is clinically referred to as epistaxis. This occurs when the fragile capillaries within the nasal passages or the upper respiratory tract rupture. The appearance can range from a faint pink tinged spray to distinct red droplets. While often caused by inflammation or infection, it indicates that the nasal lining is compromised and requires professional evaluation.

Is my cat sneezing blood an emergency?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours if the symptoms are not immediate.
  • Proceed to an emergency animal hospital immediately if the bleeding is continuous.
  • Seek emergency care if your cat is struggling to breathe or if their gums appear pale or blue.
  • Avoid monitoring the symptom at home for more than 48 hours.

How can taking a photo help with my cat's triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the blood droplets on the floor or furniture to help the vet assess blood volume and color.
  • Photograph your cat's face to show whether the discharge is coming from one or both nostrils.
  • Provide visual evidence to help the veterinary team identify potential causes like foreign bodies, dental issues, or infections.
  • Use photos to show how fresh the blood is before the physical exam begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats that present with sneezing and blood droplets, differential diagnoses to consider include oronasal neoplasia, dental disease with oronasal fistula, nasal foreign body, and nasopharyngeal disease such as a foreign body or mass lesion. Diagnostic steps include a thorough history and physical examination, complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, urinalysis, and, if epistaxis is present, a coagulation profile including prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, buccal mucosal bleeding time, and platelet count. Imaging of the nasal chambers via nasal or dental radiographs, or preferably CT, is warranted. Rhinoscopy, retroflex nasopharyngoscopy, nasal flushing, and nasal biopsy should be performed after imaging to avoid iatrogenic changes. Further diagnostics involve deep nasal tissue culture and serum titers for cryptococcal antigen. Unilateral nasal discharge may suggest a foreign body, while a chronic nasal discharge that becomes bilateral or changes character from mucopurulent to serosanguineous or hemorrhagic may indicate neoplastic or mycotic disease.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: Can a cold cause my cat to sneeze blood?
faqs.answer: Yes, severe upper respiratory infections can cause enough inflammation and tissue irritation to result in minor bleeding when the cat sneezes.

faqs.question: Could a dental problem be the cause?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Cats have very long tooth roots that sit close to the nasal cavity. A tooth root abscess can cause inflammation that leads to sneezing and bloody discharge.

faqs.question: What should I do if the sneezing won't stop?
faqs.answer: If your cat is having a sneezing fit that won't resolve, keep them in a calm, cool environment to lower their blood pressure and contact a vet immediately to prevent further nasal trauma.
